// Who I Am

I’m a PhD Candidate specialising in competition economics at Université de Bourgogne, working at CESAER. My work combines causal inference, machine learning, and network analysis to study collusive behaviour. I conduct econometric analysis to support antitrust enforcement and policy design.








// Research Network


Competition · Markets · Enforcement


 Network Active

title: Markets, Conspirancy & Enforcement
text:
Markets, Conspirancy & Enforcement


Why competition fails? How firms interact? Whay happens if we intervene the market?


Scroll to explore






I’m an economist who studies how companies behave when they’re supposed to compete, and what happens when they don’t . My research focuses on two industries that affect everyone: electricity and construction. Here is what I’ve found.







Chapter 01 Energy Markets


Can AI Catch Cheatersin Electricity Markets?


With Martin Huber & Jeremy Proz







A machine learning algorithm can detect secret price-fixing in energy markets faster and more reliably than traditional investigation, by spotting patterns that are invisible to the human eye.




The problem. In electricity markets, energy companies sometimes secretly coordinate their bids or deliberately hold back power they could provide to push prices up. These cartels cost consumers and businesses billions, but they’re notoriously hard to catch. Traditional investigations are slow, reactive, and expensive.


What we did. We applied machine learning the same technology that filters your spam and flags suspicious credit card transactions to bidding records from the Italian electricity market. The algorithm scans thousands of bids and automatically flags suspicious patterns that suggest collusion, far faster than any human analyst could.


The novelty. We also developed novel signals to detect capacity withholding: when producers deliberately offer less power than they actually have available, creating artificial scarcity to drive up prices. These signals come from the day-ahead market, where data is more transparent and timely.


The finding. The algorithm successfully identified known cartel behavior in both test cases. This is a proof of concept: automated, data-driven surveillance could fundamentally change how regulators monitor energy markets catching cheaters earlier and at far lower cost.




2 Confirmed cartelcases tested


ML Supervised learningfor cartel detection


Italy Wholesale electricitymarket studied








Chapter 02 Market Geography


The Hidden Mapof Competition


With Julie LeGallo & Jean Dubé







Firms don’t compete along official administrative borders. Real competition follows its own invisible geography and mapping it reveals 47 natural markets that look nothing like the official map.




The problem. When regulators decide whether a market is competitive, they need to draw boundaries around it. Typically they use official regions cities, counties, provinces. But do companies actually compete across those lines? Or do they have their own invisible territories shaped by distance, roads, and local knowledge?


What we did. Using eight years of bid data from Quebec’s municipal road paving contracts, I built a network showing which companies competed with each other based on where they actually chose to bid. I then applied tools borrowed from social network analysis to find natural clusters: groups of firms genuinely fighting over the same turf.


The finding. We identified 47 distinct competitive areas that don’t match Quebec’s administrative map at all. Distance matters enormously: double the distance between two firms and you roughly halve the chance they’ll bid on the same project. Competition is intensely local but locally it follows roads and economic geography, not political lines.


Why it matters. Regulators using official boundaries may be misjudging market concentration: missing local monopolies in some areas while overestimating competition in others. Better market maps lead to better antitrust policy.




47 Functional marketareas identified


8 yrs Of bidding datafrom Québec


−1 Distance elasticityof competition








Chapter 03 Antitrust Policy


Did Intervention Work or Do Firms Keep Business as Usual?


Job Market Paper







Antitrust intervention changed bidding behavior in Québec’s road construction sector but the effects were temporary, not permanent. Breaking a cartel matters, but may not be enough on its own.




The problem. For years, road construction companies in Québec secretly agreed on who would win which contract a classic cartel that cost taxpayers hundreds of millions of dollars. When authorities launched an antitrust inquiry and dismantled the cartel, a key question remained: did that actually make the market more competitive in the long run?


What I did. I tracked bidding behavior across hundreds of Québec municipalities from 2011 to 2022 before, during, and after the antitrust intervention. To isolate the true effect of the crackdown from everything else happening in the economy at the same time, I used a statistical method that makes treated and untreated municipalities more comparable before comparing them.


The finding. Yes, behavior changed after the intervention bids became more competitive initially. But those effects were temporary rather than lasting. Over time, the market settled back toward patterns that resembled the pre-intervention era. The cartel was broken, but the underlying structural features of the market its geographic fragmentation, high barriers to entry, and small number of local players remained.


The lesson. One-time enforcement actions, however powerful, may not produce lasting competitive change. Sustained oversight combined with structural reforms that lower barriers to entry may be needed to keep markets genuinely competitive after a cartel is dissolved.




12 yrs Of data tracked2011 – 2022


DiD Difference-in-differenceswith entropy balance


? Long-run effectsstill uncertain






Why This Research Matters

Markets Are Not Neutral


Competition in markets affects all of us through electricity bills, the quality of our roads, and how public money gets spent. When companies cheat, we all pay. My research shows that these problems can be detected with new tools, that enforcement can help, and that we still have much to learn about making markets work for everyone.

title: Research
text:
Machine Learning for Detecting Collusion and Capacity Withholding in Wholesale Electricity Markets


Forthcoming, with Martin Huber and Jeremy Proz



Collusion and capacity withholding in electricity wholesale markets are important mechanisms of market manipulation. This study applies a refined machine learning–based cartel-detection algorithm to two cartel cases in the Italian electricity market and evaluates its out-of-sample performance. We propose novel screens capturing the capacity-withholding behavior of electricity providers and find that including such screens—derived from the day-ahead spot market rather than the balancing market—can improve cartel detection. Overall, the study provides a proof of concept for the strong potential of supervised machine learning techniques to detect and dismantle cartels in electricity markets.



 PDF  Slides





The Spatial Structure of Competitive Interactions: Evidence from Public Procurement Networks in Quebec


Working Paper, with Julie LeGallo and Jean Dubé



How do local markets form spatially? Using bid-level data from Quebec’s municipal paving sector (2012–2019), we construct a geographical bidder overlap (GBO) index measuring supply-side substitutability between postal codes. Gravity-style regressions show that GBO decays with distance (elasticity ≈ −1) and increases with location similarity. Community detection on the GBO network identifies 47 functional market areas that diverge substantially from administrative boundaries. Exponential Random Graph models indicate that transitivity, geographic proximity, and agglomeration are each associated with market integration. The 47 functional market areas diverge substantially from administrative boundaries, suggesting that standard regional units are imperfect proxies for competitive geography in this sector.



 PDF  Slides







Business as Usual: The Case of the Antitrust Inquiry in Quebec, Canada


Job Market Paper



What are the dynamic effects of antitrust intervention? This paper examines the dynamic effects of antitrust intervention on bidding behavior in Quebec’s paving sector between 2011 and 2022. Using tender data, I analyze how antitrust intervention shapes competition across different levels of market aggregation. I implement a difference-in-differences with entropy balance weights to improve comparability between treated and untreated municipalities. I complement my study with a panel event design to trace the evolution of treatment effects over time. Although the estimates are imprecise due to structural features of the data, the results suggest that the impact of antitrust intervention was temporary rather than persistent, with limited long-run effects on public procurement outcomes. Nevertheless, the evidence points to a shift in the competitive landscape following the dismantling of the cartel.



 PDF  Slides

title: Ivan Marin
text:
// A Story About Markets, Conspiration and Key Players


Imagine you organise a contest The same players always seem to win … again and again. You tell yourself it might be normal… But there are some patterns, something feels off, noncompetitive allegations emerge So you call a detective.



Scroll to explore






// The Detective

I’m that detective


I’m an economist who studies how companies behave when they’re supposed to compete, and what happens when they don’t . My research focuses on two industries that affect everyone: electricity and construction. Here is what I’ve found.









// Research Network


Competition · Markets · Enforcement


 Looking for the key player…










Energy Markets


Can AI Catch Cheatersin Electricity Markets?


With Martin Huber & Jeremy Proz  ·  Forthcoming



An AI trained to spot suspicious patterns caught electricity cartels that took investigators years to uncover — automatically, and far faster.




The problem. Energy companies sometimes secretly agree to push prices up — either by coordinating their bids or by deliberately holding back power they could provide, creating fake scarcity. These schemes cost consumers billions. They’re hard to detect, and traditional investigations are slow and expensive.


What we did. We applied machine learning — the same kind of technology that filters your spam or flags a suspicious credit card transaction — to bidding records from the Italian electricity market. The algorithm scans thousands of bids and flags suspicious patterns automatically. It successfully identified two known cartels. A proof of concept that automated surveillance could catch cheaters earlier, and at far lower cost.



2 cartel cases tested Italian electricity market Supervised ML










Market Geography


Real Competition Doesn’tFollow the Official Map


With Julie LeGallo & Jean Dubé  ·  Working Paper



Companies don’t compete along political borders. Mapping real competition revealed 47 natural markets that look nothing like the official ones — and some regions are far less competitive than regulators think.




The problem. When regulators decide whether a market is competitive, they typically draw boundaries using official regions — cities, provinces. But do companies actually compete across those lines? Or do they have invisible territories shaped by distance and roads?


What we found. Using eight years of bidding data from Québec’s road paving sector, we built a map of where real competition happens. The result: 47 natural market areas that don’t match the official map at all. Distance is everything — double the distance between two companies and you roughly halve the chance they’ll ever compete. Regulators drawing the wrong boundaries may be missing local monopolies hiding in plain sight.



47 markets discovered 8 years of data Québec










Antitrust Policy


When Authorities Crack Down —Does It Actually Last?


Job Market Paper



Cracking down on a cartel changed behaviour — but the effects were temporary, not permanent. Breaking a cartel matters, but it may not be enough on its own.




The problem. For years, road construction companies in Québec secretly divided up public contracts among themselves — a cartel that cost taxpayers hundreds of millions of dollars. When authorities launched an investigation and dismantled it, a key question remained: did the crackdown actually make the market more competitive in the long run?


What I found. I tracked bidding behaviour across Québec municipalities for 12 years — before, during, and after the intervention. The crackdown worked at first: bids became more competitive. But over time, the market drifted back toward old patterns. The cartel was broken, but the underlying conditions — geographic fragmentation, few local players, high barriers to entry — were still there. One-time enforcement may not be enough without structural reform.



12 years tracked Hundreds of municipalities Job Market Paper






// Why This Matters

Markets Are Not Neutral


Competition affects all of us — through electricity bills, road quality, and how public money gets spent. When companies cheat, we all pay. My research shows these problems can be detected with new tools, that enforcement can help, and that we still have much to learn about keeping markets genuinely open.
